Inferences for mean/proportion

Let’s recap what a confidence interval is,

A confidence interval of level 100(1−α)%100(1 - \alpha)\%100(1−α)% means that we are 100(1−α)%100(1 - \alpha)\%100(1−α)% confident that the true value of the parameter is included into the interval.

When dealing with confidence intervals, we will often encounter different “types” of situations. Let’s review these.

Confidence Interval on the mean of a Normal Distribution, variance known

Suppose we have a normal distribution with unknown mean μ\muμ and known variance σ2\sigma^2σ2.

We have thus a random sample X1,X2,…,XnX_1, X_2, \ldots, X_nX1​,X2​,…,Xn​, such that, for all iii,

Xi∼N(μ,σ2)X_i \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)Xi​∼N(μ,σ2)

with μ\muμ unknown and σ\sigmaσ a known constant.

We would like a confidence interval for μ\muμ.

We know that,

Xˉ=1n∑i=1nXi∼N(μ,σ2n)\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i = 1}^n X_i \sim N\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)Xˉ=n1​i=1∑n​Xi​∼N(μ,nσ2​)

we can standardize this to,

Z=Xˉ−μσ/n=nXˉ−μσ∼N(0,1)Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1)Z=σ/n​Xˉ−μ​=n​σXˉ−μ​∼N(0,1)

So, because Z∼N(0,1)Z \sim N(0, 1)Z∼N(0,1),

P(−z1−α/2≤Z≤z1−α/2)=1−αP(-z_{1 - \alpha/2} \leq Z \leq z_{1 - \alpha/2}) = 1 - \alphaP(−z1−α/2​≤Z≤z1−α/2​)=1−α

Thus,

P(−z1−α/2≤nXˉ−μσ≤z1−α/2)=1−αP\left(-z_{1 - \alpha/2} \leq \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma} \leq z_{1 - \alpha/2}\right) = 1 - \alphaP(−z1−α/2​≤n​σXˉ−μ​≤z1−α/2​)=1−α

Re-arranging this, we get,

P(Xˉ−z1−α/2σn≤μ≤Xˉ+z1−α/2σn)=1−αP\left(\bar{X} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{X} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alphaP(Xˉ−z1−α/2​n​σ​≤μ≤Xˉ+z1−α/2​n​σ​)=1−α

Let’s call them LLL and UUU for lower and upper, finally, the interval is,

[L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2σn,xˉ+z1−α/2σn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2​n​σ​,xˉ+z1−α/2​n​σ​]

Confidence Interval on the mean of an arbitrary distribution, variance known

Let us recap the central limit theorem.

The Central Limit (CLT) implies if nnn is large enough,

Z=nXˉ−μσ∼N(0,1)Z = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1)Z=n​σXˉ−μ​∼N(0,1)

Thus,

P(−z1−α/2≤nXˉ−μσ≤z1−α/2)≃1−αP\left(-z_{1 - \alpha/2} \leq \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{\sigma} \leq z_{1 - \alpha/2}\right) \simeq 1 - \alphaP(−z1−α/2​≤n​σXˉ−μ​≤z1−α/2​)≃1−α

Which yields the (same) interval,

[L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2σn,xˉ+z1−α/2σn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2​n​σ​,xˉ+z1−α/2​n​σ​]

Confidence interval on the mean of a normal distribution, variance unknown

In the case we do not have the variance, we need to sample it!

Recall the sample variance,

S2=1n−1∑i=1n(Xi−Xˉ)2S^2 = \frac{1}{n - 1} \sum_{i = 1}^n (X_i - \bar{X})^2S2=n−11​i=1∑n​(Xi​−Xˉ)2

A natural procedure is thus to,

T=nXˉ−μST = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{S}T=n​SXˉ−μ​

In a normal population, the exact distribution of TTT is T∼tn−1T \sim t_{n - 1}T∼tn−1​.

We can write,

P(−tn−1;1−α/2≤nXˉ−μS≤tn−1;1−α/2)=1−αP(-t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \leq \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu}{S} \leq t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2}) = 1 - \alphaP(−tn−1;1−α/2​≤n​SXˉ−μ​≤tn−1;1−α/2​)=1−α

or,

P(Xˉ−tn−1;1−α/2Sn≤μ≤Xˉ+tn−1;1−α/2Sn)=1−αP\left(\bar{X} - t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}} \leq \mu \leq \bar{X} + t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{S}{\sqrt{n}}\right) = 1 - \alphaP(Xˉ−tn−1;1−α/2​n​S​≤μ≤Xˉ+tn−1;1−α/2​n​S​)=1−α

Which yields us the interval,

[L,U]=[xˉ−tn−1;1−α/2sn,xˉ+tn−1;1−α/2sn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−tn−1;1−α/2​n​s​,xˉ+tn−1;1−α/2​n​s​]

In large samples,

T≃N(0,1)T \simeq N(0, 1)T≃N(0,1)

Consequently, an approximate confidence interval of level 100(1−α)%100(1 - \alpha)\%100(1−α)% for μ\muμ is,

[L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2sn,xˉ+z1−α/2sn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2​n​s​,xˉ+z1−α/2​n​s​]

Confidence Interval on the mean: Summary

Is the population normal?

  • If yes, is σ\sigmaσ known?
    • If yes, use an **exact zzz-confidence interval:
      • [L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2σn,xˉ+z1−α/2σn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2​n​σ​,xˉ+z1−α/2​n​σ​]
    • If no, use an **exact ttt-confidence interval:
      • [L,U]=[xˉ−tn−1;1−α/2sn,xˉ+tn−1;1−α/2sn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−tn−1;1−α/2​n​s​,xˉ+tn−1;1−α/2​n​s​]
  • If no, use an approximate large sample confidence interval:
    • [L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2σn,xˉ+z1−α/2σn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2​n​σ​,xˉ+z1−α/2​n​σ​]
    • or
    • [L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2sn,xˉ+z1−α/2sn][L, U] = \left[\bar{x} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}, \bar{x} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\right][L,U]=[xˉ−z1−α/2​n​s​,xˉ+z1−α/2​n​s​]

Confidence Interval on the proportion

The random variable to study is,

X={1If the individual has the characteristic of interest0If notX = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{If the individual has the characteristic of interest} \newline 0 & \text{If not} \end{cases}X={10​If the individual has the characteristic of interestIf not​

X1,X2,…,XnX_1, X_2, \ldots, X_nX1​,X2​,…,Xn​ is a set of nnn independent Bern(ppp) random variables.

Thus,

Y=∑i=1nXi∼B(n,p)Y = \sum_{i = 1}^n X_i \sim B(n, p)Y=i=1∑n​Xi​∼B(n,p)

and the sample proportion is,

P^=Yn\hat{P} = \frac{Y}{n}P^=nY​

We also know that,

nP^−pp(1−p)∼N(0,1)\sqrt{n} \frac{\hat{P} - p}{\sqrt{p(1 - p)}} \sim N(0, 1)n​p(1−p)​P^−p​∼N(0,1)

if nnn is large.

Approximate two-sided confidence interval of level 100(1−α)%100(1 - \alpha)\%100(1−α)% for ppp is given by,

[p^−z1−α/2p^(1−p^)n,p^+z1−α/2p^(1−p^)n]\left[\hat{p} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}}, \hat{p} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}}\right][p^​−z1−α/2​np^​(1−p^​)​​,p^​+z1−α/2​np^​(1−p^​)​​]

Hypotheses testing for the mean

Let’s recall hypothesis testing. The null hypothesis is usually of the form,

H0:μ=μ0H_0 : \mu = \mu_0H0​:μ=μ0​

The alternative hypothesis can be a two-sided alternative,

Ha:μ≠μ0H_a : \mu \neq \mu_0Ha​:μ=μ0​

or one-sided alternatives,

Ha:μ>μ0orHa:μ<μ0H_a : \mu > \mu_0 \quad \text{or} \quad H_a : \mu < \mu_0Ha​:μ>μ0​orHa​:μ<μ0​

Remember, we have two important cases

  1. Rejecting H0H_0H0​ when it is true: type I error.
  2. Failing to reject H_0 when it is false: type II error.
P(Type I error)=P(reject H0∣H0 is true )=αP(Type II error)=P(fail to reject H0∣H0 is false )=βP(\text{Type I error}) = P(\text{reject } H_0 | H_0 \text{ is true }) = \alpha \newline P(\text{Type II error}) = P(\text{fail to reject } H_0 | H_0 \text{ is false }) = \betaP(Type I error)=P(reject H0​∣H0​ is true )=αP(Type II error)=P(fail to reject H0​∣H0​ is false )=β

Note that β\betaβ depends on the (unknown) value of μ\muμ under the alternative.

Assume for the moment that the population is normal with known σ\sigmaσ.

Xˉ∼N(μ,σ2n)\bar{X} \sim N(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n})Xˉ∼N(μ,nσ2​)

At significance level α\alphaα, we are after two constants ℓ\ellℓ and uuu such that,

α=P(Xˉ∉[ℓ,u] when μ=μ0)=P(Z∉[nℓ−μ0σ,nu−μ0σ])\alpha = P(\bar{X} \notin [\ell, u] \text{ when } \mu = \mu_0) = P\left(Z \notin \left[\sqrt{n} \frac{\ell - \mu_0}{\sigma}, \sqrt{n} \frac{u - \mu_0}{\sigma}\right]\right)α=P(Xˉ∈/[ℓ,u] when μ=μ0​)=P(Z∈/[n​σℓ−μ0​​,n​σu−μ0​​])

Thus,

nℓ−μ0σ=zα/2=−z1−α/2\sqrt{n} \frac{\ell - \mu_0}{\sigma} = z_{\alpha/2} = -z_{1 - \alpha/2}n​σℓ−μ0​​=zα/2​=−z1−α/2​

and,

nu−μ0σ=z1−α/2\sqrt{n} \frac{u - \mu_0}{\sigma} = z_{1 - \alpha/2}n​σu−μ0​​=z1−α/2​

This yields,

ℓ=μ0−z1−α/2σnu=μ0+z1−α/2σn.\ell = \mu_0 - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} \newline u = \mu_0 + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}.ℓ=μ0​−z1−α/2​n​σ​u=μ0​+z1−α/2​n​σ​.

The decision rule is then,

Reject H0 if xˉ∉[μ0−z1−α/2σn,μ0+z1−α/2σn]\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x} \notin [\mu_0 - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \mu_0 + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}]Reject H0​ if xˉ∈/[μ0​−z1−α/2​n​σ​,μ0​+z1−α/2​n​σ​]

Hypotheses testing for the mean: ppp-value

The ppp-value is the probability that the test statistic will take on a value that is at least as extreme as the observed value when H0H_0H0​ is true. (extreme is to be understood in the direction of the alternative).

When testing H0:μ=μ0H_0 : \mu = \mu_0H0​:μ=μ0​ against Ha:μ≠μ0H_a : \mu \neq \mu_0Ha​:μ=μ0​, the ppp-value will be the probability of finding the random variable Xˉ\bar{X}Xˉ more different to μ0\mu_0μ0​ than the observed xˉ\bar{x}xˉ, that is,

p=P(Xˉ∉[μ0±∣xˉ−μ0∣] when μ=μ0)=1−P(Xˉ∈[μ0±∣xˉ−μ0∣] when μ=μ0)\begin{align*} p & = P(\bar{X} \notin [\mu_0 \pm |\bar{x} - \mu_0|] \text{ when } \mu = \mu_0) \newline & = 1 - P(\bar{X} \in [\mu_0 \pm |\bar{x} - \mu_0|] \text{ when } \mu = \mu_0) \end{align*}p​=P(Xˉ∈/[μ0​±∣xˉ−μ0​∣] when μ=μ0​)=1−P(Xˉ∈[μ0​±∣xˉ−μ0​∣] when μ=μ0​)​

Let us define,

z0=nxˉ−μ0σz_0 = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma}z0​=n​σxˉ−μ0​​

as the “observed value of the test statistic”.

As we know that Z=nXˉ−μ0σ∼N(0,1)Z = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1)Z=n​σXˉ−μ0​​∼N(0,1), we can write,

p=1−P(nXˉ−μ0σ∈[nμ0±∣xˉ−μ0∣−μ0σ])=1−P(Z∈[−∣z0∣,∣z0∣])=2(1−ϕ(∣z0∣))\begin{align*} p & = 1 - P(\sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma} \in \left[\sqrt{n} \frac{\mu_0 \pm |\bar{x} - \mu_0| - \mu_0}{\sigma}\right]) \newline & = 1 - P(Z \in [-|z_0|, |z_0|]) = 2(1 - \phi(|z_0|)) \end{align*}p​=1−P(n​σXˉ−μ0​​∈[n​σμ0​±∣xˉ−μ0​∣−μ0​​])=1−P(Z∈[−∣z0​∣,∣z0​∣])=2(1−ϕ(∣z0​∣))​

Operationally, since a ppp-value is computed, we typically compare it to a predefined significance level α\alphaα to make a decision:

{if p<α, reject H0if p≥α, do not reject H0\begin{cases} \text{if } p < \alpha, \text{ reject } H_0 \newline \text{if } p \geq \alpha, \text{ do not reject } H_0 \end{cases}{if p<α, reject H0​if p≥α, do not reject H0​​

Hypotheses testing for the mean: one-sided

With Ha:μ>μ0H_a : \mu > \mu_0Ha​:μ>μ0​ we are after a constant uuu such that,

P(Xˉ>u when μ=μ0)=αP(\bar{X} > u \text{ when } \mu = \mu_0) = \alphaP(Xˉ>u when μ=μ0​)=α

As we know that Z=nXˉ−μ0σ∼N(0,1)Z = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma} \sim N(0, 1)Z=n​σXˉ−μ0​​∼N(0,1), the decision rule is reject H0H_0H0​ if xˉ>μ0+z1−ασn\bar{x} > \mu_0 + z_{1 - \alpha} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}xˉ>μ0​+z1−α​n​σ​.

Again with z0=nxˉ−μ0σz_0 = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma}z0​=n​σxˉ−μ0​​, the ppp-value is,

p=P(Xˉ>xˉ when μ=μ0)=P(Z>nxˉ−μ0σ)=1−ϕ(z0)p = P(\bar{X} > \bar{x} \text{ when } \mu = \mu_0) = P(Z > \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma}) = 1 - \phi(z_0)p=P(Xˉ>xˉ when μ=μ0​)=P(Z>n​σxˉ−μ0​​)=1−ϕ(z0​)

With Ha:μ<μ0H_a : \mu < \mu_0Ha​:μ<μ0​, we are after a constant ℓ\ellℓ such that,

P(Xˉ<ℓ when μ=μ0)=αP(\bar{X} < \ell \text{ when } \mu = \mu_0) = \alphaP(Xˉ<ℓ when μ=μ0​)=α

The decision rule is reject H0H_0H0​ if xˉ<μ0−z1−ασn\bar{x} < \mu_0 - z_{1 - \alpha} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}xˉ<μ0​−z1−α​n​σ​.

The ppp-value is,

p=P(Xˉ<xˉ when μ=μ0)=P(Z<nxˉ−μ0σ)=ϕ(z0)p = P(\bar{X} < \bar{x} \text{ when } \mu = \mu_0) = P(Z < \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma}) = \phi(z_0)p=P(Xˉ<xˉ when μ=μ0​)=P(Z<n​σxˉ−μ0​​)=ϕ(z0​)

Hypotheses testing for the mean: other cases

Say we have a normal population with unknown standard deviation.

Specifically, for the two-sided test H0:μ=μ0H_0 : \mu = \mu_0H0​:μ=μ0​ against Ha:μ≠μ0H_a : \mu \neq \mu_0Ha​:μ=μ0​, the decision rule is,

reject H0 if xˉ∉[μ0−tn−1;1−α/2sn,μ0+tn−1;1−α/2sn]\text{reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x} \notin [\mu_0 - t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}, \mu_0 + t_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}]reject H0​ if xˉ∈/[μ0​−tn−1;1−α/2​n​s​,μ0​+tn−1;1−α/2​n​s​]

and from the observed value of the test statistic,

t0=nxˉ−μ0st_0 = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s}t0​=n​sxˉ−μ0​​

we can compute the ppp-value,

p=1−P(T∈[−∣t0∣,∣t0∣])=2P(T>∣t0∣) where T∼tn−1p = 1 - P(T \in [-|t_0|, |t_0|]) = 2P(T > |t_0|) \text{ where } T \sim t_{n - 1}p=1−P(T∈[−∣t0​∣,∣t0​∣])=2P(T>∣t0​∣) where T∼tn−1​

If we have non-normal populations with known or unknown standard deviation,

reject H0 if xˉ∉[μ0−z1−α/2σn,μ0+z1−α/2σn]\text{reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x} \notin \left[\mu_0 - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}, \mu_0 + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}\right]reject H0​ if xˉ∈/[μ0​−z1−α/2​n​σ​,μ0​+z1−α/2​n​σ​]

or,

reject H0 if xˉ∉[μ0−z1−α/2sn,μ0+z1−α/2sn]\text{reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x} \notin \left[\mu_0 - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}, \mu_0 + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}\right]reject H0​ if xˉ∈/[μ0​−z1−α/2​n​s​,μ0​+z1−α/2​n​s​]

The associated approximate ppp-value will be given by,

p=2(1−ϕ(∣z0∣)),p = 2(1 - \phi(|z_0|)),p=2(1−ϕ(∣z0​∣)),

with z0=nxˉ−μ0σz_0 = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma}z0​=n​σxˉ−μ0​​ or z0=nxˉ−μ0sz_0 = \sqrt{n} \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s}z0​=n​sxˉ−μ0​​.

Hypotheses testing for the proportion

Large-sample confidence interval for ppp,

[p^−z1−α/2p^(1−p^)n,p^+z1−α/2p^(1−p^)n]\left[\hat{p} - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}}, \hat{p} + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})}{n}}\right][p^​−z1−α/2​np^​(1−p^​)​​,p^​+z1−α/2​np^​(1−p^​)​​]

The decision rule at (approximate) level α\alphaα is,

reject H0 if p^∉[p0−z1−α/2p0(1−p0)n,p0+z1−α/2p0(1−p0)n]\text{reject } H_0 \text{ if } \hat{p} \notin \left[p_0 - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{p_0(1 - p_0)}{n}}, p_0 + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{p_0(1 - p_0)}{n}}\right]reject H0​ if p^​∈/[p0​−z1−α/2​np0​(1−p0​)​​,p0​+z1−α/2​np0​(1−p0​)​​]

The (approximate) ppp-value for this test is,

p=2(1−ϕ(∣z0∣))p = 2(1 - \phi(|z_0|))p=2(1−ϕ(∣z0​∣))

where z0=np^−p0p0(1−p0)z_0 = \sqrt{n} \frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1 - p_0)}}z0​=n​p0​(1−p0​)​p^​−p0​​.

R code for finding z1−α/2z_{1 - \alpha/2}z1−α/2​

Example

Suppose 53 people among 100 surveyed is for the proposition, find the 95% confidence interval for the proportion of people in favor of the proposition.

Built-in functions in R for finding the CI of proportion estimate

Built-in functions in R for finding the CI of mean estimate

Inferences for difference of means

In many situations it is quite common to be interested in *comparing two ‘populations’ in regard to a parameter of interest.

The two ‘populations’ may be:

  • Produced items using an existing and a new technique.
  • Success rates in two groups of individuals.
  • Health test results for patients who received a drug and for patients who received a placebo.

Two-sample test

X11,X12,…,X1n1X_{11}, X_{12}, \ldots, X_{1n_1}X11​,X12​,…,X1n1​​ is a sample from population 1. X21,X22,…,X2n2X_{21}, X_{22}, \ldots, X_{2n_2}X21​,X22​,…,X2n2​​ is a sample from population 2.

The samples are independent (i.e., observations in sample 1 are by no means linked to the observations in sample 2, they concern different individuals).

What we would like to know is whether μ1=μ2\mu_1 = \mu_2μ1​=μ2​ or not.

So,

H0:μ1=μ2H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2H0​:μ1​=μ2​

We compute the sample means xˉ1\bar{x}_1xˉ1​ and xˉ2\bar{x}_2xˉ2​.

  • If xˉ1≃xˉ2\bar{x}_1 \simeq \bar{x}_2xˉ1​≃xˉ2​, then H_0 is probably acceptable.
  • If xˉ1\bar{x}_1xˉ1​ is considerably different to xˉ2\bar{x}_2xˉ2​, that is evidence that H0H_0H0​ is not true and we are tempted to reject it.

Note that the alternative hypothesis can be,

H1:μ1≠μ2 ∣ two-sided alternativeH_1 : \mu_1 \neq \mu_2 \ | \ \text{two-sided alternative}H1​:μ1​=μ2​ ∣ two-sided alternative

or,

H1:μ1>μ2orH1:μ1<μ2 ∣ one-sided alternativeH_1 : \mu_1 > \mu_2 \quad \text{or} \quad H_1 : \mu_1 < \mu_2 \ | \ \text{one-sided alternative}H1​:μ1​>μ2​orH1​:μ1​<μ2​ ∣ one-sided alternative

We know that,

X1ˉ=1n1∑i=1n1X1i∼N(μ1,σ12n1)\bar{X_1} = \frac{1}{n_1} \sum_{i = 1}^{n_1} X_{1i} \sim N\left(\mu_1, \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1}\right)X1​ˉ​=n1​1​i=1∑n1​​X1i​∼N(μ1​,n1​σ12​​)

and,

X2ˉ=1n2∑i=1n2X2i∼N(μ2,σ22n2)\bar{X_2} = \frac{1}{n_2} \sum_{i = 1}^{n_2} X_{2i} \sim N\left(\mu_2, \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}\right)X2​ˉ​=n2​1​i=1∑n2​​X2i​∼N(μ2​,n2​σ22​​)

we deduce the sampling distribution of X1ˉ−X2ˉ\bar{X_1} - \bar{X_2}X1​ˉ​−X2​ˉ​,

X1ˉ−X2ˉ∼N(μ1−μ2,σ12n1+σ22n2)\bar{X_1} - \bar{X_2} \sim N\left(\mu_1 - \mu_2, \frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}\right)X1​ˉ​−X2​ˉ​∼N(μ1​−μ2​,n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​)

Now, testing for H0:μ1=μ2H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2H0​:μ1​=μ2​ exactly amounts to for H0:μ1−μ2=0H_0 : \mu_1 - \mu_2 = 0H0​:μ1​−μ2​=0, with X1ˉ−X2ˉ\bar{X_1} - \bar{X_2}X1​ˉ​−X2​ˉ​ as an estimator for μ1−μ2\mu_1 - \mu_2μ1​−μ2​.

Two-sample test: known variances

Suppose that σ1\sigma_1σ1​ and σ2\sigma_2σ2​ are known.

For the two-sided test (with Ha:μ1−μ2≠0H_a : \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0Ha​:μ1​−μ2​=0), at significance level α\alphaα, the decision rule is,

Reject H0 if x1ˉ−x2ˉ∉[−z1−α/2σ12n1+σ22n2,z1−α/2σ12n1+σ22n2]\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2} \notin \left[-z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}, z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}\right]Reject H0​ if x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​∈/​−z1−α/2​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​,z1−α/2​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​​

ppp-value is,

p=2(1−ϕ(∣z0∣)),p = 2(1 - \phi(|z_0|)),p=2(1−ϕ(∣z0​∣)),

where z0=xˉ1−xˉ2σ12n1+σ22n2z_0 = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{\sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}}z0​=n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​xˉ1​−xˉ2​​.

Similarly, for the one-sided test HA:μ1>μ2H_A : \mu_1 > \mu_2HA​:μ1​>μ2​, the decision rule is,

reject H0 if x1ˉ−x2ˉ>z1−ασ12n1+σ22n2\text{reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2} > z_{1 - \alpha} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}reject H0​ if x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​>z1−α​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​

and the ppp-value is,

p=1−ϕ(z0)p = 1 - \phi(z_0)p=1−ϕ(z0​)

while for the one-sided test HA:μ1<μ2H_A : \mu_1 < \mu_2HA​:μ1​<μ2​, the decision rule is,

reject H0 if x1ˉ−x2ˉ<−z1−ασ12n1+σ22n2\text{reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2} < -z_{1 - \alpha} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}reject H0​ if x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​<−z1−α​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​

and the ppp-value is,

p=ϕ(z0)p = \phi(z_0)p=ϕ(z0​)

100(1−α)%100(1 - \alpha)\%100(1−α)% two-sided confidence interval for μ1−μ2\mu_1 - \mu_2μ1​−μ2​ is,

[(x1ˉ−x2ˉ)−z1−α/2σ12n1+σ22n2,(x1ˉ−x2ˉ)+z1−α/2σ12n1+σ22n2]\left[(\bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2}) - z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}, (\bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2}) + z_{1 - \alpha/2} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}\right]​(x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​)−z1−α/2​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​,(x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​)+z1−α/2​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​​

100(1−α)%100(1 - \alpha)\%100(1−α)% one-sided confidence intervals for μ1−μ2\mu_1 - \mu_2μ1​−μ2​ are,

(−∞,(x1ˉ−x2ˉ)+z1−ασ12n1+σ22n2]\left(-\infty, (\bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2}) + z_{1 - \alpha} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}\right]​−∞,(x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​)+z1−α​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​​

and,

[(x1ˉ−x2ˉ)−z1−ασ12n1+σ22n2,+∞]\left[(\bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2}) - z_{1 - \alpha} \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{\sigma_2^2}{n_2}}, +\infty\right]​(x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​)−z1−α​n1​σ12​​+n2​σ22​​​,+∞​

Two-sample test: unknown equal variances

Assume now σ1=σ2=σ\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigmaσ1​=σ2​=σ, but σ\sigmaσ is unknown.

We can estimate σ2\sigma^2σ2 by the pooled variance estimator,

Sp2=∑i=1n1(X1i−X1ˉ)2+∑i=1n2(X2i−X2ˉ)2n1+n2−2S_{p}^2 = \frac{\sum_{i = 1}^{n_1} \left(X_{1i} - \bar{X_1}\right)^2 + \sum_{i = 1}^{n_2} \left(X_{2i} - \bar{X_2}\right)^2}{n_1 + n_2 - 2}Sp2​=n1​+n2​−2∑i=1n1​​(X1i​−X1​ˉ​)2+∑i=1n2​​(X2i​−X2​ˉ​)2​

Where S12S_1^2S12​ and S22S_2^2S22​ are the sample variances of the two samples,

S12=1n1−1∑i=1n1(X1i−X1ˉ)2S_1^2 = \frac{1}{n_1 - 1} \sum_{i = 1}^{n_1} (X_{1i} - \bar{X_1})^2S12​=n1​−11​i=1∑n1​​(X1i​−X1​ˉ​)2

and,

S22=1n2−1∑i=1n2(X2i−X2ˉ)2S_2^2 = \frac{1}{n_2 - 1} \sum_{i = 1}^{n_2} (X_{2i} - \bar{X_2})^2S22​=n2​−11​i=1∑n2​​(X2i​−X2​ˉ​)2

For the two-sided test (with Ha:μ1−μ2≠0H_a : \mu_1 - \mu_2 \neq 0Ha​:μ1​−μ2​=0), at significance level α\alphaα, at significance level α\alphaα, the decision rule is,

reject H0 if x1ˉ−x2ˉ∉[−tn1+n2−2;1−α/2sp1n1+1n2,tn1+n2−2;1−α/2sp1n1+1n2]\text{reject } H_0 \text{ if } \bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2} \notin \left[-t_{n_1 + n_2 - 2;1 - \alpha/2} s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}, t_{n_1 + n_2 - 2;1 - \alpha/2} s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}\right]reject H0​ if x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​∈/[−tn1​+n2​−2;1−α/2​sp​n1​1​+n2​1​​,tn1​+n2​−2;1−α/2​sp​n1​1​+n2​1​​]

The ppp-value is given by,

p=2P(T>∣t0∣)p = 2P(T > |t_0|)p=2P(T>∣t0​∣)

with T∼tn1+n2−2T \sim t_{n_1 + n_2 - 2}T∼tn1​+n2​−2​, and where t0t_0t0​ is the observed value of the test statistic,

t0=xˉ1−xˉ2sp1n1+1n2t_0 = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}}t0​=sp​n1​1​+n2​1​​xˉ1​−xˉ2​​

This test is known as the two-sample ttt-test.

A 100(1−α)%100(1 - \alpha)\%100(1−α)% two-sided confidence interval for μ1−μ2\mu_1 - \mu_2μ1​−μ2​ is,

[(x1ˉ−x2ˉ)−tn1+n2−2;1−α/2sp1n1+1n2,(x1ˉ−x2ˉ)+tn1+n2−2;1−α/2sp1n1+1n2]\left[(\bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2}) - t_{n_1 + n_2 - 2;1 - \alpha/2} s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}, (\bar{x_1} - \bar{x_2}) + t_{n_1 + n_2 - 2;1 - \alpha/2} s_p \sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1} + \frac{1}{n_2}}\right][(x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​)−tn1​+n2​−2;1−α/2​sp​n1​1​+n2​1​​,(x1​ˉ​−x2​ˉ​)+tn1​+n2​−2;1−α/2​sp​n1​1​+n2​1​​]

Two-sample test: unknown unequal variances

There is no exact result available. An approximate result can be applied,

(X1ˉ−X2ˉ)−(μ1−μ2)S12n1+S22n2∼tν\frac{(\bar{X_1} - \bar{X_2}) - (\mu_1 - \mu_2)}{\sqrt{\frac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{S_2^2}{n_2}}} \sim t_{\nu}n1​S12​​+n2​S22​​​(X1​ˉ​−X2​ˉ​)−(μ1​−μ2​)​∼tν​

where the number of degrees of freedom is,

ν=(S12n1+S22n2)2(S12n1)2n1−1+(S22n2)2n2−1\nu = \frac{\left(\frac{S_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{S_2^2}{n_2}\right)^2}{\frac{\left(\frac{S_1^2}{n_1}\right)^2}{n_1 - 1} + \frac{\left(\frac{S_2^2}{n_2}\right)^2}{n_2 - 1}}ν=n1​−1(n1​S12​​)2​+n2​−1(n2​S22​​)2​(n1​S12​​+n2​S22​​)2​

(rounded down to the nearest integer).

This is called Welch’s two sample ttt-test.

Example

6 subjects were given a drug (treatment group, μ2\mu_2μ2​) and an additional 6 subjects a placebo (control group, μ1\mu_1μ1​). Their reaction time to a stimulus was measured (in ms). Perform a two sample ttt-test for comparing the means of the treatment and control groups

Let’s use a one-sided test.

H0:μ1=μ2vsH1:μ1<μ2H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2 \quad \text{vs} \quad H_1 : \mu_1 < \mu_2H0​:μ1​=μ2​vsH1​:μ1​<μ2​

We can also do a Welch test,

Paired data for difference of means

Two sample ttt-test cannot be used when we deal with “before and after” data, and numerous situations where the data are naturally paired (and thus, not independent).

Let (X11,X21),(X12,X22),…,(X1n,X2n)(X_{11}, X_{21}), (X_{12}, X_{22}), \ldots, (X_{1n}, X_{2n})(X11​,X21​),(X12​,X22​),…,(X1n​,X2n​) be a random sample of nnn pairs of observations drawn from two subpopulations X1X_1X1​ and X2X_2X2​, with respective means μ1\mu_1μ1​ and μ2\mu_2μ2​.

An easy way is just to consider the diffrences.

Di=Xi1−Xi2D_i = X_{i1} - X_{i2}Di​=Xi1​−Xi2​

We have just a sample D1,D2,…,DnD_1, D_2, \ldots, D_nD1​,D2​,…,Dn​ from a distribution with mean,

μD=μ1−μ2\mu_D = \mu_1 - \mu_2μD​=μ1​−μ2​

Testing for H0:μ1=μ2H_0 : \mu_1 = \mu_2H0​:μ1​=μ2​ is just H0:μD=0H_0 : \mu_D = 0H0​:μD​=0. This can be accomplished by performing the usual one sample test for mean.

So, in R we can,

Inferences for variance

We know that,

S2=1n−1∑i=1n(Xi−Xˉ)2S^2 = \frac{1}{n - 1} \sum_{i = 1}^n (X_i - \bar{X})^2S2=n−11​i=1∑n​(Xi​−Xˉ)2

and is a natural estimator for the population variance σ2\sigma^2σ2.

In general, little can be said about the distribution of S2S^2S2. However, when the population is normal, that is X∼N(μ,σ2)X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)X∼N(μ,σ2), then,

(n−1)S2σ2∼χn−12\frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n - 1}σ2(n−1)S2​∼χn−12​

Let χν;α2\chi^2_{\nu; \alpha}χν;α2​ be the value such that,

P(X>χν;α2)=αP(X > \chi^2_{\nu; \alpha}) = \alphaP(X>χν;α2​)=α

for X∼χν2X \sim \chi^2_{\nu}X∼χν2​.

As we know that,

(n−1)S2σ2∼χn−12\frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n - 1}σ2(n−1)S2​∼χn−12​

we can write,

P(χn−1;1−α/22≤(n−1)S2σ2≤χn−1;α/22)=1−αP(\chi^2_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \leq \frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \leq \chi^2_{n - 1;\alpha/2}) = 1 - \alphaP(χn−1;1−α/22​≤σ2(n−1)S2​≤χn−1;α/22​)=1−α

which can be re-arranged to,

P((n−1)S2χn−1;α/22≤σ2≤(n−1)S2χn−1;1−α/22)=1−αP\left(\frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\chi^2_{n - 1;\alpha/2}} \leq \sigma^2 \leq \frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\chi^2_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2}}\right) = 1 - \alphaP(χn−1;α/22​(n−1)S2​≤σ2≤χn−1;1−α/22​(n−1)S2​)=1−α

Which gives us the interval,

[(n−1)S2χn−1;α/22,(n−1)S2χn−1;1−α/22]\left[\frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\chi^2_{n - 1;\alpha/2}}, \frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\chi^2_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2}}\right][χn−1;α/22​(n−1)S2​,χn−1;1−α/22​(n−1)S2​]

Now, H0:σ2=σ02H_0 : \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2H0​:σ2=σ02​ against Ha:σ2≠σ02H_a : \sigma^2 \neq \sigma_0^2Ha​:σ2=σ02​. It is natural to reject H0H_0H0​ whenever s2s^2s2 is too distant from σ02\sigma_0^2σ02​.

We are after two constants ℓ\ellℓ and uuu such that,

α=P(S2∉[ℓ,u] when σ2=σ02)=P((n−1)S2σ02∉[(n−1)S2u,(n−1)S2ℓ])\alpha = P(S^2 \notin [\ell, u] \text{ when } \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2) = P\left(\frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2} \notin \left[\frac{(n - 1)S^2}{u}, \frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\ell}\right]\right)α=P(S2∈/[ℓ,u] when σ2=σ02​)=P(σ02​(n−1)S2​∈/[u(n−1)S2​,ℓ(n−1)S2​])

This yields,

ℓ=χn−1;α/22σ02n−1u=χn−1;1−α/22σ02n−1\ell = \frac{\chi^2_{n - 1;\alpha/2} \sigma_0^2}{n - 1} \newline u = \frac{\chi^2_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \sigma_0^2}{n - 1}ℓ=n−1χn−1;α/22​σ02​​u=n−1χn−1;1−α/22​σ02​​

The decision rule is then,

Reject H0 if s2∉[χn−1;α/22σ02n−1,χn−1;1−α/22σ02n−1]\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } s^2 \notin \left[\frac{\chi^2_{n - 1;\alpha/2} \sigma_0^2}{n - 1}, \frac{\chi^2_{n - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \sigma_0^2}{n - 1}\right]Reject H0​ if s2∈/[n−1χn−1;α/22​σ02​​,n−1χn−1;1−α/22​σ02​​]

One-sided CI,

[0,(n−1)S2χn−1;α2][(n−1)S2χn−1;1−α2,+∞]\left[0, \frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\chi^2_{n - 1;\alpha}}\right] \newline \left[\frac{(n - 1)S^2}{\chi^2_{n - 1;1 - \alpha}}, +\infty\right][0,χn−1;α2​(n−1)S2​][χn−1;1−α2​(n−1)S2​,+∞]

One-sided test,

For Ha:σ2>σ02, reject H0 if s2>χn−1;α2σ02n−1For Ha:σ2<σ02, reject H0 if s2<χn−1;1−α2σ02n−1\text{For } H_a : \sigma^2 > \sigma_0^2, \text{ reject } H_0 \text{ if } s^2 > \frac{\chi^2_{n - 1;\alpha} \sigma_0^2}{n - 1} \newline \text{For } H_a : \sigma^2 < \sigma_0^2, \text{ reject } H_0 \text{ if } s^2 < \frac{\chi^2_{n - 1;1 - \alpha} \sigma_0^2}{n - 1}For Ha​:σ2>σ02​, reject H0​ if s2>n−1χn−1;α2​σ02​​For Ha​:σ2<σ02​, reject H0​ if s2<n−1χn−1;1−α2​σ02​​

The ppp-value is,

P(S2>s2)=1−P((n−1)S2σ02≤(n−1)s2σ02)=1−P(χn−12≤(n−1)s2σ02)P(S2<s2=P((n−1)S2σ02≤(n−1)s2σ02)=P(χn−12≤(n−1)s2σ02)P(S^2 > s^2) = 1 - P\left(\frac{(n - 1) S^2}{\sigma^2_0} \leq \frac{(n - 1) s^2}{\sigma^2_0}\right) = 1 - P\left(\chi^2_{n - 1} \leq \frac{(n - 1) s^2}{\sigma^2_0}\right) \newline P(S^2 < s^2 = P\left(\frac{(n - 1) S^2}{\sigma^2_0} \leq \frac{(n - 1) s^2}{\sigma^2_0}\right) = P\left(\chi^2_{n - 1} \leq \frac{(n - 1) s^2}{\sigma^2_0}\right)P(S2>s2)=1−P(σ02​(n−1)S2​≤σ02​(n−1)s2​)=1−P(χn−12​≤σ02​(n−1)s2​)P(S2<s2=P(σ02​(n−1)S2​≤σ02​(n−1)s2​)=P(χn−12​≤σ02​(n−1)s2​)

Inferences for ratio of variances/test of equality of variances

Let X11,X12,…,X1nX_{11}, X_{12}, \ldots, X_{1n}X11​,X12​,…,X1n​ is a sample from population 1. Let X21,X22,…,X2nX_{21}, X_{22}, \ldots, X_{2n}X21​,X22​,…,X2n​ is a sample from population 2.

The samples are independent, and we would like to whether σ12=σ22\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2σ12​=σ22​ or not.

Define the sample variances,

S12=1n1−1∑i=1n1(X1i−X1ˉ)2S22=1n2−1∑i=1n2(X2i−X2ˉ)2S_1^2 = \frac{1}{n_1 - 1} \sum_{i = 1}^{n_1} (X_{1i} - \bar{X_1})^2 \newline S_2^2 = \frac{1}{n_2 - 1} \sum_{i = 1}^{n_2} (X_{2i} - \bar{X_2})^2S12​=n1​−11​i=1∑n1​​(X1i​−X1​ˉ​)2S22​=n2​−11​i=1∑n2​​(X2i​−X2​ˉ​)2

We can use the test statistic,

F=S12S22F = \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2}F=S22​S12​​

In general, there is no known exact distribution for F. Fortunately, if X1i∼N(μ1,σ12)X_{1i} \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)X1i​∼N(μ1​,σ12​) and X2i∼N(μ2,σ22)X_{2i} \sim N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)X2i​∼N(μ2​,σ22​), F has an F(n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1) distribution if the null (σ12=σ22\sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2σ12​=σ22​) is true.

H0:σ12=σ22H_0 : \sigma_1^2 = \sigma_2^2H0​:σ12​=σ22​ against Ha:σ12≠σ22H_a : \sigma_1^2 \neq \sigma_2^2Ha​:σ12​=σ22​.

It is natural to reject H0H_0H0​ whenever FFF is too big or too small,

The decision rule is,

Reject H0 if F∉[1Fn1−1,n2−1;1−α/2,Fn1−1,n2−1;α/2]\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } F \notin \left[\frac{1}{F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;1 - \alpha/2}}, F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;\alpha/2}\right]Reject H0​ if F∈/[Fn1​−1,n2​−1;1−α/2​1​,Fn1​−1,n2​−1;α/2​]

Two-sided CI for σ12σ22\frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2}σ22​σ12​​,

P(Fn1−1,n2−1;1−α/2≤σ12σ22≤Fn1−1,n2−1;α/2)=1−αP(F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;1 - \alpha/2} \leq \frac{\sigma_1^2}{\sigma_2^2} \leq F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;\alpha/2}) = 1 - \alphaP(Fn1​−1,n2​−1;1−α/2​≤σ22​σ12​​≤Fn1​−1,n2​−1;α/2​)=1−α

Which yields us the interval,

[S12S22Fn1−1,n2−1;α/2,S12S22Fn1−1,n2−1;1−α/2]\left[\frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2 F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;\alpha/2}}, \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2 F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;1 - \alpha/2}}\right][S22​Fn1​−1,n2​−1;α/2​S12​​,S22​Fn1​−1,n2​−1;1−α/2​S12​​]

One-sided CI,

[0,S12S22Fn1−1,n2−1;α][S12S22Fn1−1,n2−1;1−α,+∞]\left[0, \frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2 F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;\alpha}}\right] \newline \left[\frac{S_1^2}{S_2^2 F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;1 - \alpha}}, +\infty\right][0,S22​Fn1​−1,n2​−1;α​S12​​][S22​Fn1​−1,n2​−1;1−α​S12​​,+∞]

One-sided test,

For Ha:σ12>σ22, reject H0 if F>Fn1−1,n2−1;1−αFor Ha:σ12<σ22, reject H0 if F<Fn1−1,n2−1;α\text{For } H_a : \sigma_1^2 > \sigma_2^2, \text{ reject } H_0 \text{ if } F > F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;1 - \alpha} \newline \text{For } H_a : \sigma_1^2 < \sigma_2^2, \text{ reject } H_0 \text{ if } F < F_{n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1;\alpha}For Ha​:σ12​>σ22​, reject H0​ if F>Fn1​−1,n2​−1;1−α​For Ha​:σ12​<σ22​, reject H0​ if F<Fn1​−1,n2​−1;α​

The ppp-value is,

P(F>f)=1−P(F(n1−1,n2−1)≤f)P(F<f)=P(F(n1−1,n2−1)≤f)P(F > f) = 1 - P(F(n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1) \leq f) \newline P(F < f) = P(F(n_1 - 1, n_2 - 1) \leq f)P(F>f)=1−P(F(n1​−1,n2​−1)≤f)P(F<f)=P(F(n1​−1,n2​−1)≤f)
